scenario_name: Scenario 14 — Anti-Pattern Detection in Existing Architecture
justification: All 6 pass criteria met: (1) All 5 anti-patterns identified by name — shared database, synchronous fan-in, god endpoint, HTTP-triggered background jobs, direct cross-service DB reads. (2) Prioritized remediation plan with 5 phases ordered by risk. (3) Shared database and direct DB reads identified as critical/high severity. (4) REST-based jobs identified as fragile with specific failure modes (no retry, no concurrency control, timeout mismatch). (5) Trade-offs for fixing the shared database explicitly covered (cross-service queries harder, eventual consistency, operational overhead of multiple DB instances). (6) Incremental remediation via Strangler Fig pattern, explicitly warns against big-bang cutover.

observations.text: All 5 anti-patterns identified with detailed analysis of why each is harmful, including second-order effects
Clear prioritized remediation sequence with rationale for ordering — broker first enables later phases
Shared database and direct DB reads correctly identified as critical/high severity
REST-as-job-trigger anti-pattern thoroughly analyzed with specific failure modes (concurrency, timeouts, observability gaps)
Comprehensive trade-offs section covering all major architectural decisions with alternatives considered
Strangler Fig pattern recommended for incremental migration — explicitly avoids big-bang rewrite
Detailed domain model with bounded contexts and context map showing upstream/downstream relationships
Risk section identifies migration-specific risks with concrete mitigations
Remediation priority ordering differs slightly from rubric — places Notification fan-in as Phase 1 over shared DB, though the rationale (broker enables later phases) is sound
Output is extremely verbose for an architecture review — could be more concise while retaining the same substance

agent_output_excerpt:
## Executive Summary

This system exhibits five classic distributed systems anti-patterns that have compounded over 3 years of organic growth. The most critical issues are the shared database between Order and Courier services (destroying service autonomy) and the synchronous Notification fan-in (creating a system-wide availability bottleneck). Left unaddressed, these patterns will produce cascading failures, data corruption risks, and increasingly painful deployments. The remediation plan is se
